title: Can treatment with chondroitin and glucosamine sulphate prevent changes in the articular disc caused by temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis?
research_question: Can treatment with chondroitin and glucosamine sulphate prevent changes in the articular disc caused by temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis?
methods_summary: 36 male rabbits divided into control, osteoarthritis, and treatment groups
Osteoarthritis induced by intra-articular injection of sodium monoiodoacetate
Treatment group received subcutaneous chondroitin and glucosamine sulphate (7.5 mg/kg) every 3 days starting 10 days post-induction
Collagen quantification via biochemical assay (Sircol assay) and histological analysis (PicroSirius Red staining under polarized light)
Serum TNF-α levels measured by enzyme immunoassay (ELISA)
Statistical analysis using two-way ANOVA and repeated measures ANOVA
dataset: 36 male New Zealand rabbits (Oryctolagus cuniculus L.), 4 months old, 3-4 kg
key_findings: Treatment group showed significantly increased total collagen area in articular disc compared to control and osteoarthritis groups
No significant difference in collagen concentration between groups
Serum TNF-α levels were significantly lower in treatment group compared to osteoarthritis group post-treatment
Histological analysis showed better preservation of collagen fiber organization in treatment group
extracted_insights: CGS treatment delayed collagen degeneration in TMJ articular disc and reduced serum TNF-α levels, suggesting a preventive and anti-inflammatory effect on OA progression, potentially by maintaining collagen fiber organization and modulating inflammatory cytokines.
